title: Orientation
contents:
This course curates some of my favorite topics at the intersection of data science and data visualization. I’ve tried selecting readings that are both intellectually stimulating and useful for real-world data science projects.
Course Goals
The official learning objectives are,
Reshape and tidy data for use in data visualizations.
Use visual encodings and layout to support exploratory analysis and data communication.
Design visualizations to summarize the results of dimensionality reduction and clustering algorithms.
Implement visualizations of geospatial, tree-structured, and network data.
Use visualizations to diagnose and inspect complex statistical and machine learning models.
Prototype custom visualizations using the ggplot2 library in R and vega-lite library in javascript.
Spot and remove chartjunk.
More informally, my intent is to help you gain hands-on experience working with different types visualization techniques, so that you emerge from this course with confidence in your data science problem solving skills.
Something I believe strongly is that, to be an effective scientist, you don’t have to be a genius, you just have to look and notice well (To be a great scientist, you also need to know where to look). In any case, in 2020, it can be powerful to be fluent in data visualization.
Recurring Tasks
I will send a course announcement every Sunday through Canvas, giving upcoming tasks for the week, along with their deadlines. The recurring tasks include,
Readings: Each week has 2 - 3 readings from textbooks or papers, and these are key to learning the course material well.
Lectures: My lectures will summarize the readings and give further examples. They will be linked in the “Lectures” table on the home page.
Homeworks: These are due on Canvas every other Friday. It’s not enough to understand visualization abstractly – the only way to improve is to practice using different tools and visual idioms.
Group project: You will have four milestones, culminating in a final project report.
Course Materials
This website contains reading and lecture content. For interaction and feedback, we will use Canvas. Here are some links to keep in mind,
Website
Reading schedule
Recordings and notes

Canvas
Weekly announcements
Homework and project assignments
Piazza forum
Office hour / Discussion links
Midterms
Grades

title: Introduction to ggplot2
contents:
Reading: Introduction to Data Science, Chapter 7
[Recording]
Rmarkdown
ggplot2 is an R implementation of the Grammar of Graphics. The idea of the Grammar is to define the basic “words” from which visualizations are built, and then let users compose them in original ways. This is in contrast to systems with prespecified chart types, where the user is forced to pick from a limited dropdown menu of plots. Just like ordinary language, once you know how to speak the grammar of graphics, you will be able to express ideas that no one else has had before.
These are libraries we’ll use in this lecture.



Components of a Graph
Your reading for this lecture constructs this plot, and we’re going to recreate it in these notes.



Every ggplot2 plot is made from three components,
Data: This is the data.frame that we want to visualize.
Geometry: These are the types of visual marks that appear on the plot.
Aesthetic Mapping: This links the data with the visual marks.
The Data
Let’s load up the data. Each row is an observation, and each column is an attribute that describes the observation. This is important because each mark that you see on a ggplot – a line, a point, a tile, … – had to start out as a row within an R data.frame. The visual properties of the mark (e.g., color) are determined by the values along columns. These type of data are often referred to as “tidy” data, and we’ll have a full week discussing this topic.
Here’s an example of the data above in tidy format,

       state abb region population total
1    Alabama  AL  South    4779736   135
2     Alaska  AK   West     710231    19
3    Arizona  AZ   West    6392017   232
4   Arkansas  AR  South    2915918    93
5 California  CA   West   37253956  1257
6   Colorado  CO   West    5029196    65

This is one example of how the same information might be stored in a non-tidy way, making visualization much harder.

            Alabama   Alaska  Arizona Arkansas California Colorado
abb              AL       AK       AZ       AR         CA       CO
region        South     West     West    South       West     West
population  4779736   710231  6392017  2915918   37253956  5029196
total           135       19      232       93       1257       65
           Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia  Florida
abb                 CT       DE                   DC       FL
region       Northeast    South                South    South
population     3574097   897934               601723 19687653
total               97       38                   99      669
            Georgia   Hawaii    Idaho      Illinois       Indiana
abb              GA       HI       ID            IL            IN
region        South     West     West North Central North Central
population  9920000  1360301  1567582      12830632       6483802
total           376        7       12           364           142
                    Iowa        Kansas Kentucky Louisiana     Maine
abb                   IA            KS       KY        LA        ME
region     North Central North Central    South     South Northeast
population       3046355       2853118  4339367   4533372   1328361
total                 21            63      116       351        11
           Maryland Massachusetts      Michigan     Minnesota
abb              MD            MA            MI            MN
region        South     Northeast North Central North Central
population  5773552       6547629       9883640       5303925
total           293           118           413            53
           Mississippi      Missouri  Montana      Nebraska   Nevada
abb                 MS            MO       MT            NE       NV
region           South North Central     West North Central     West
population     2967297       5988927   989415       1826341  2700551
total              120           321       12            32       84
           New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico  New York
abb                   NH         NJ         NM        NY
region         Northeast  Northeast       West Northeast
population       1316470    8791894    2059179  19378102
total                  5        246         67       517
           North Carolina  North Dakota          Ohio Oklahoma
abb                    NC            ND            OH       OK
region              South North Central North Central    South
population        9535483        672591      11536504  3751351
total                 286             4           310      111
             Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina
abb              OR           PA           RI             SC
region         West    Northeast    Northeast          South
population  3831074     12702379      1052567        4625364
total            36          457           16            207
            South Dakota Tennessee    Texas     Utah   Vermont
abb                   SD        TN       TX       UT        VT
region     North Central     South    South     West Northeast
population        814180   6346105 25145561  2763885    625741
total                  8       219      805       22         2
           Virginia Washington West Virginia     Wisconsin  Wyoming
abb              VA         WA            WV            WI       WY
region        South       West         South North Central     West
population  8001024    6724540       1852994       5686986   563626
total           250         93            27            97        5

Often, one of the hardest parts in making a ggplot2 plot is not coming up with the right ggplot2 commands, but reshaping the data so that it’s in a tidy format.
Geometry
The words in the grammar of graphics are the geometry layers. We can associate each row of a data frame with points, lines, tiles, etc., just by referring to the appropriate geom in ggplot2. A typical plot will compose a chain of layers on top of a dataset,

ggplot(data) + [layer 1] + [layer 2] + …

For example, by deconstructing the plot above, we would expect to have point and text layers. For now, let’s just tell the plot to put all the geom’s at the origin.



You can see all the types of geoms in the cheat sheet. We’ll be experimenting with a few of these in the third lecture this week.
Aesthetic mappings
Aesthetic mappings make the connection between the data and the geometry. The physical properties of a geom’s mark is determined by values in the corresponding observation’s data field, as specified by the aesthetic mapping.
Deconstructing the original graph further, we recognize these links between the abstract data fields for each state and the circles’ visual properties,
State Population <–> \(x\)-axis coordinate
Number of murders <–> \(y\)-axis coordinate
Geographical region <–> color
We can establish this mapping in the plot using the aes function. Notice that we don’t need to quote the column names within this command – it knows to refer back to the murders data frame in ggplot(murders).



The original plot used a log-scale. To transform the x and y axes, we can use scales.



Once nuance is that scales aren’t limited to these sort of \(x\) and \(y\) transformations. They can be applied to modify any relationship between a data field and its appearance on the page. For example, this changes the mapping between the region field and circle color.



The problem with this graph is that it doesn’t allow us to compare individual states, only regions. For that visual task, we’ll need text labels. We can encode the coordinates for these marks again using aes, but this time within a geom_text layer.



Note that each type of layer uses different visual properties to encode the data – we could pass in label to a geom_text label, but that wouldn’t make sense for a geom_point layer.
It’s usually a good thing to make your code as concise as possible. For ggplot2, one way you can accomplish this is by sharing elements from the aes mapping across layers (e.g., not having to type population and total twice). This can be done by defining a “global” aesthetic, putting it inside the top ggplot call.



Finishing touches
How can we improve the readability of this plot? From the intro figure, you might already have ideas,
Prevent labels from overlapping. It’s impossible to read some of the state names.
Add a line showing the national rate. This serves as a point of reference, allowing us to see whether an individual state is above or below the national murder rate.
Give meaningful axis / legend labels and a title.
Move the legend to the top of the figure. Right now, we’re wasting a lot of visual real estate in the right hand side, just to let people know what each color means.
Use a better color theme.
For 1., the ggrepel package can handle the computation needed to find better state name positions.



For 2., let’s first compute the national murder rate (per million),

[1] 3.034555e-05

Now, we can use this as the slope in a geom_abline layer, which encodes a slope and intercept as a line on a graph.



For 3., we can add a labs layer to write labels and a theme to reposition the legend. I used unit_format from the scales package to change the scientific notation in the \(x\)-axis labels to something more readable.



For 5., I find the gray background with reference lines a bit distracting. We can simplify the appearance using theme_bw. I also like the colorbrewer palette, which can be used by calling a different color scale.



Some bonus exercises, which will train you to look at your graphics more carefully, as well as build your familiarity with ggplot2.
Try reducing the size of the text labels. Hint: use the size argument in geom_text_repel.
Increase the size of the circles in the legend. Hint: Use override.aes within a guide.
Re-order the regions in the legend. Hint: Reset the factor levels in the region field of the murders data.frame.
Only show labels for a subset of states that are far from the national rate. Hint: Filter the murders data.frame, and use the data field specific to the geom_text_repel layer.

title: Introduction to Vega-Lite
contents:
Reading: Data Types, Graphical Marks, and Visual Encoding Channels
[Recording]
Rmarkdown
Observable.
You can think of vega-lite as the javascript equivalent of ggplot2. It also provides a grammar of graphics, where layers can be composed on top of one another. The main reason for knowing vega-lite (opposed to just using ggplot2 for everything) is that it can support flexible interaction. Also, it’s a good gateway to understanding richer data visualization packages, like d3.
I encourage you to follow along in a new observable



Components of a Graph
We’re going to construct this graph, which is from this week’s reading, step-by-step, just like in the ggplot2 lecture. I realize that this is a bit repetitive, but I want to make it obvious just how similar ggplot2 and vega-lite are.


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":4,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
Data and Geometry
First, let’s load up and preview the data. We’re using the arquero library (aq) to make it easier to manage data in javascript – the filtering and printing steps would be more difficult without it.
data = aq.fromCSV(await FileAttachment("gapminder.csv").text()) // I used the file upload button to add this file
data2000 = data.filter(d => d.year == 2000) // filter to only 2000
data2000.view()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":6,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
and create a basic visual mark, without any encodings. Like in our first ggplot2 figure, the mark is collapsed into a single point at the origin. Notice the analogy betwen vega-lite’s markPoint() and ggplot2’s geom_point().
vl.markPoint()
  .data(data2000)
  .render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":7,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
Encodings
Let’s deconstruct the original figure and see how data fields associated with each country get mapped to which visual properties of the corresponding point.
Fertility <–> \(x\)-axis coordinate
Life expectancy <–> \(y\)-axis coordinate
Country cluster <–> Point color
Population <–> Point size
We can build this up one step at a time. To encode the fertility and life expectancy fields, we use vl.x() and vl.y() in an encode() call. One subtlety is that it’s necessary to specify the type of the field through the – fieldQ means that the field is “Quantitative”. We’ll get into more detail about specifying data types in the next lecture.
vl.markPoint()
  .data(data2000)
  .encode(
    vl.x().fieldQ("fertility"), // what happens if you change this to fieldN()?
    vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect")
  )
  .render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":8,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
The same idea can be used to encode the country cluster and population fields. Note that I use fieldN to specify that cluster is a Nominal variable.
Hint: How do you know which commands encode which fields? Until it becomes second nature, it’s good to keep the vega-lite-api and vega-lite documentation close at hand.
vl.markPoint()
  .data(data2000)
  .encode(
    vl.x().fieldQ("fertility"),
    vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect"),
    vl.color().fieldN("cluster"), // what happens if you change this to fieldO()?
    vl.size().fieldQ("pop")
  )
  .render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":9,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
Finishing touches
We’ve made headway in our visualization task, but a more critical eye will help us improve the display. It will also give us an initial look at interactivity. The main things we ought to improve in this display are,
The open rings are a bit distracting. They violate the principle of Smallest Effective Difference.
Some of the points are impossibly small.
The axis labels are still the names from the data.frame.
There are distractingly many levels in the legend for population size.
We can’t actually tell which country each point corresponds to. For this, we will add a tooltip (a label that appears when you mouseover the point).
For (1), let’s replace the open rings with filled circles. We can pass options to markPoint in the same way that we passed options outside of aes to ggplot geom layers. For (2), we can modify the default size range by adding on a .scale() to the vl.size() encoding – this is similar to adding a custom scale in ggplot2.
vl.markPoint({filled: true})
.data(data2000)
.encode(
  vl.x().fieldQ("fertility"),
  vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect"),
  vl.color().fieldN("cluster"),
  vl.size().fieldQ("pop").scale({range: [25, 1000]}) // try changing the ranges
)
.render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":10,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
For (3), we can add a .title() to fields for which we want to customize titles, and for (4), we can modify the tick count in the .legend() for size.
vl.markPoint({filled: true})
.data(data2000)
.encode(
  vl.x().fieldQ("fertility").title("Fertility"),
  vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect").title("Life Expectancy"),
  vl.color().fieldN("cluster"),
  vl.size().fieldQ("pop").scale({range: [25, 1000]}).legend({tickCount: 3}).title("Population"),
)
.render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":11,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
Now to (5). The idea is to think of a tooltip as another type of encoding – just like coordinate positions and colors, a label is a way of transforming a field in an abstract dataset into a visual property we can observe on a screen.
vl.markPoint({filled: true})
.data(data2000)
.encode(
  vl.x().fieldQ("fertility").title("Fertility"),
  vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect").title("Life Expectancy"),
  vl.color().fieldN("cluster"),
  vl.size().fieldQ("pop").scale({range: [25, 1000]}).legend({tickCount: 3}).title("Population"),
  vl.tooltip().fieldN("country"),
  vl.order().fieldQ('pop').sort('descending') // if you remove this, large points prevent mouseover on the points below them
)
.render()


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":12,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
One point I want you to take away from both this and the ggplot2 introductions is that building a plot is an iterative process, similar in many ways to writing. Learn to read your plots critically and improve them step-by-step.
Finally, for a taste of what’s to come, here’s a version of the same plot that let’s us compare many years over time.


{"x":{"notebook":"@krisrs1128/introduction-to-vega-lite","include":13,"hide":null,"input":null,"observers":null,"update_height":true,"update_width":true},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}
Don’t worry about the code implementation, but in case you’re curious, it’s only a few extra lines over what we’ve already implemented.
{
  // define a slider selector object
  let selectYear = vl.selectSingle("select").fields("year")
    .init({year: 1955})
    .bind(vl.slider().min(1955).max(2005).step(5).name("Year"))
  
  // update the plot depending on the value of the slider
  return vl.markPoint({filled: true})
    .data(data)
    .select(selectYear) // refers to the slider bar
    .transform(vl.filter(selectYear))
    .encode(
      vl.x().fieldQ("fertility").title("Fertility").scale({domain: [0, 9]}), // hard code the axes ranges
      vl.y().fieldQ("life_expect").title("Life Expectancy").scale({domain: [0, 90]}),
      vl.color().fieldN("cluster"),
      vl.size().fieldQ("pop").scale({domain: [0, 1200000000], range: [25, 1000]}).legend({tickCount: 3}).title("Population"),
      vl.tooltip().fieldN("country"),
      vl.order().fieldQ('pop').sort('descending')
    )
    .render()
}

title: Types, Marks, and Encodings
contents:
Reading: Data Types, Graphical Marks, and Visual Encoding Channels
[Recording]
Rmarkdown
The reason that the ggplot2 and vega-lite packages feel similar to use is that they are based on the same fundamental concepts. Each observation (row) within a dataset corresponds to a geometric mark in a visualization. Important attributes (columns) of an observation within a dataset can be encoded by properties of visual marks.
In fact, we can roughly translate the function names between the two packages,
geom_* <–> .mark*
aes <–> vl.encode()
scale_* <–> .scale()
labs <–> .title()
A good visualization makes it easy to visually compare the relevant attributes of the relevant observations. The difficulty of visualization is that there are often many choices for the marks and encodings to use for any given comparison. It’s also difficult to know which comparisons are actually of interest. For these reasons, we’re going to want to build up a vocabulary of marks and encodings.

Field types
One immediate restriction on the types of encodings that make sense for a given field comes from the “type” of that field.
Nominal: These are fields that allow identity comparisons (is \(A = B\)?) between observations, but don’t come with any notion of order.
City region, country name, movie genres, file types, …
Encodings: Position, color hue, shape, motion

Ordinal: These are fields that allow ordering comparisons (is \(A > B\)?) between observations, but don’t let you quantify the degree of difference.
Shirt size, sports team rankings, order of appearance of characters on TV show
Encodings: Position, size (radius, length, area), color brightness are candidates, though need to avoid inappropriate arithmetic comparisons

Quantitative: These are field types that let you make distance comparisons between observations. For interval data, absolute comparisons can be made (\(A - B\)), while for ratio data, only relative ones (\(\frac{A}{B}\)) are possible.
Temperature, stock price, number of drinks sold in a coffee shop per day
Encodings: Position, size (radius, length, area), color brightness

This is not an exhaustive list, and there are subtleties, * The same field could be treated as nominal, ordinal, or quantitative depending on the context. Age could be nominal (underage or overage), ordinal (teenager, \(20 - 30\), …), or quantitative. * A given field might be thought of hierarchically. There might be interesting patterns at the city, county, or region level – what seems like a single field in your dataset can become the source for three derived fields. * Even though temporal fields can be thought of as quantitative, they come with their own language. For example, the hour - week - month hierarchy is a natural one for temporal data.
It’s worth highlighting that, even if a particular encoding could be used for a given data type, different encodings have different effectiveness. When trying to encoding several data fields at once, a choice of one encoding over another will implicitly prioritize certain comparisons over others.
For example, in the figure below, the same two numbers are encoded using many different visual properties – position on a shared \(y\)-axis, position on distinct \(y\)-axes, etc. – and study participants were asked to gauge the difference in the numbers. People were best at comparing positions on a common scale, and worst at distinguishing differences in areas.

Here is a similar example. In the left four panels, a nominal field is encoded using color (left two) and shape (middle two). The red circles are easiest to find in the left two. In the right two panels, two nominal fields are encoded, using both shape and color. It’s much harder to find the red circle – you have to scan over the entire image to find it, which you didn’t have to do at all for the first two panels.

Implicit in this discussion is that there is never any “perfect” visualization for a given dataset – the quality of a visualization is a function of its intended purpose. What comparisons do you want to facilitate? The choice of encoding will strongly affect the types of comparisons that are easy to perform.

title: Examples: Marks and Encodings
contents:
Reading: Data Types, Graphical Marks, and Visual Encoding Channels [Recording] Rmarkdown Observable Vocabulary of encodings With this in mind, it can only ever help you to have a rich vocabulary of possible visual encodings. The more kinds of marks and encodings that are at your fingertips, the better your chances are that you’ll arrive at a configuration that helps you achieve your purpose. So, let’s look at a few different types of marks and encodings in R and vega-lite. Point Marks Point marks can encode data fields using their \(x\) and \(y\) positions, color, size, and shape. The equivalent in vega-lite is vl.markPoint() .data(data2000) .encode( vl.x().fieldQ('fertility'), vl.y().fieldN('cluster'), vl.shape().fieldN('cluster') ) .render() We can specify different types of shapes using the “shape” parameter outside of the aes encoding. vl.markSquare({size: 100}) .data(data2000) .encode( vl.x().fieldQ('fertility'), vl.y().fieldN('cluster') ) .render() Bar Marks Bar marks let us associate a continuous field with a nominal one. vl.markBar() .data(data2000) .encode( vl.x().fieldN('country'), vl.y().fieldQ('pop') ) .render() To make comparisons between countries with similar populations easier, we can order them by population. To compare clusters, we can shade the bars. vl.markBar() .data(data2000) .encode( vl.x().fieldN('country').sort("-y"), vl.y().fieldQ('pop'), vl.color().field("cluster").scale({"scheme": "set2"}) ) .width(700) .render() In the plot above, each bar is anchored at 0. Instead, we could have each rectangle encode two continuous values, a start and an end. To illustrate, let’s compare the minimum and maximimum life expectancies within each country cluster. vega-lite let’s you compute the summary statistics within the encoding step. For ggplot2, we’ll need to put the summary into its own data frame. For this, we group_by each cluster, so that a summarise call finds the minimum and maximum life expectancies restricted to each cluster. # A tibble: 6 x 3 cluster min_life max_life 1 0 42.1 63.6 2 1 70.5 80.6 3 2 43.4 53.4 4 3 58.1 79.8 5 4 66.7 82 6 5 57.0 79.7 vl.markBar() .data(data2000) .encode( vl.x().min('life_expect'), vl.x2().max('life_expect'), vl.y().fieldN('cluster') ) .render() Line Marks Line marks are useful for comparing changes. Our eyes naturally focus on rates of change when we see lines. Below, we’ll plot the fertility over time, colored in by country cluster. vl.markLine({opacity: 0.7, size: 3}) .data(data) .encode( vl.x().fieldO('year'), vl.y().fieldQ('fertility'), vl.color().fieldN('cluster').scale({"scheme": "set2"}), vl.detail().fieldN("country"), vl.tooltip().fieldN('country') ) .width(500) .render() Area Marks Area marks have a flavor of both bar and line marks. The filled area supports absolute comparisons, while the changes in shape suggest derivatives. # A tibble: 6 x 3 # Groups: year [1] year cluster total_pop 1 1955 0 495927174 2 1955 1 360609771 3 1955 2 60559800 4 1955 3 355392405 5 1955 4 854125031 6 1955 5 56064015 vl.markArea() .data(data) .encode( vl.x().fieldO('year'), vl.y().sum('pop'), vl.color().fieldN('cluster').scale({"scheme": "set2"}), vl.detail().fieldN("country"), vl.tooltip().fieldN('country') ) .width(600) .render() ``` Just like in bar marks, we don't necessarily need to anchor the $y$-axis at 0. For example, here the bottom and top of each area mark is given by the 30 and 70% quartiles of population within each country cluster.
``` # A tibble: 6 x 4 # Groups: year [1] year cluster min_pop max_pop 1 1955 0 40880121. 83941368. 2 1955 1 4532940 25990229. 3 1955 2 6600426. 17377594. 4 1955 3 2221139 8671500 5 1955 4 9014491 61905422. 6 1955 5 3007625 12316126. ```
````js population_ranges = data.groupby("year", "cluster") .rollup({ // equivalent of dplyr summarise pop_low: op.quantile("pop", 0.4), pop_high: op.quantile("pop", 0.7) }) vl.markArea({"opacity": 0.7}) .data(population_ranges) .encode( vl.x().fieldO('year'), vl.y().q1('pop_low').title("Population"), vl.y2().q3('pop_high'), vl.color().fieldN('cluster').scale({"scheme": "set2"}) ) .width(600) .render()
